items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: MZ twins share what percentage of their genes?
items.why: It's 100%. “25%” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Speech production. “Speech comprehension” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What percentage of genes do DZ twins share on average?
items.why: The real figure is 50 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
